Home / FinTech / SOFI
SOFI

SoFi's Stock Got Cut in Half. The Business Never Printed Better Numbers.

14.7M members, $40.2B in deposits, a national bank charter, and a stablecoin nobody saw coming. Down 34% YTD. Something doesn't add up.

Jul 5, 2026 1 min read

SoFi's stock dropped from $27.46 to $18.24 in six months. That's a 34% haircut. Massive institutional selling, macro rotation out of fintech, and a 15.5% short interest all ganged up on the thesis. Meanwhile, the company itself just printed the best quarter of its entire existence.

That kind of disconnect doesn't happen often. And when it does, you either run for the hills or you lean in hard. Here's the case for option two.

Q1 2026 was an absolute heater. Revenue hit $3.94 billion on a trailing basis — up 42.6% year-over-year. Net income surged 134% to $166.7 million. Operating margin hit 18.3%. Profit margin hit 14.8%. SoFi is generating more income in a single quarter than it did in all of 2023 combined.

And yet the market is treating this stock like it's broken. The story here isn't complicated: SoFi added 14.7 million members, up 35% year-over-year. Total products hit 22.2 million, up 39%. Total deposits crossed $40.2 billion. Every single engagement metric is pointing up and to the right.

Start with lending, because that's where the revenue lives. Lending brought in $642 million in Q1, up 55% year-over-year. SoFi originated a record $12.2 billion in loans in a single quarter. Personal loans, student loan refinancing, home loans — they're firing on all cylinders. The bear case on lending has always been credit risk, and so far the data says SoFi knows exactly what it's doing with its underwriting models.

But lending is only part of the story. Financial Services — the non-lending side of the superapp — generated $429 million in revenue, up 41% year-over-year, with a 46% contribution margin. That's the kind of operating leverage that makes compounders. Every new member who opens a bank account, activates a debit card, or signs up for SoFi Invest feeds margin that didn't exist three years ago.

Here's the structural advantage nobody talks about enough: the bank charter. SoFi holds a national bank charter. That means it can take deposits directly without partnering with a third-party bank. That means it funds its own loans with deposits yielding near-zero cost of capital. Most fintechs are layer cakes of partnerships and middleware. SoFi owns the entire vertical stack — app, lending, banking, clearing. That's not a competitive moat. That's a fortress.

Galileo, the technology platform SoFi acquired in 2020, quietly did $75 million in revenue during Q1. That's down 27% year-over-year because they lost a major client. The knee-jerk reaction is to write Galileo off. But here's the nuance: Galileo's technology powers the card processing and account infrastructure for dozens of fintechs and neobanks. It's the pipes of the new financial system. One client loss hurts the quarterly numbers. It doesn't kill the long-term value of owning the rails.

Then there's the wildcard. SoFi launched a stablecoin called SoFiUSD in partnership with Mastercard. A fintech superapp issuing its own digital dollar, integrated with one of the largest payment networks on earth. The details are still sparse, but the direction is unmistakable. SoFi is positioning itself to exist at the intersection of traditional banking and blockchain-based finance. That's optionality that no other neobank can claim.

Anthony Noto has been saying for years that SoFi is building a one-stop financial operating system. The acquisitions tell the story — PrimaryBid for capital markets access, Peach Finance for lending automation, Composer for automated investing. They're not just adding features. They're buying the infrastructure to be the financial home for a generation that doesn't want to manage five different apps to manage their money.

The analyst consensus on SOFI is a hold — seven buys, eleven holds, three sells — with price targets ranging from $19 to $23. At $18.24, even the most conservative analyst sees some upside. Forward P/E of 22.4x on $0.81 in forward EPS? For a company growing revenue at 40% and pushing toward 15% net margins? That's not expensive. That's reasonable to cheap for the growth profile.

A 15.5% short interest on a company approaching $4 billion in trailing revenue with a bank charter, a stablecoin, and a CEO who has never missed a guidance number is the kind of setup that creates violent squeezes. Not that you should trade on squeeze potential. But it's worth noting that the crowd betting against SoFi is large, confident, and has been wrong every single quarter for the past three years.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in SOFI. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
